facts: Mars' potential ring system could be 1/3 the size of Texas
It would take 100 million basketball courts to cover the surface area
A Martian ring system could contain 1.2 billion tons of debris
idea.payoff: and we still don't know why Mars lost its rings
idea.title: Mars' Lost Rings? 🚀
idea.description: Imagine Mars with rings as stunning as Saturn's 🌌. But what if Mars actually had a ring system that rivaled our favorite gas giant? 🤯 You're about to enter a cosmic world where Mars is the new ringmaster, with a potential ring system that's 1/3 the size of Texas 🌄. Will we uncover the truth behind Mars' lost rings?

scenes.visual: A vibrant, futuristic depiction of Mars with a prominent and extensive ring system, slowly rotating against a backdrop of distant stars.
Mars' rings shown from a perspective emphasizing their vastness. A graphic overlay comparing a segment of the ring's area to an outline of the state of Texas appears briefly.
An overhead view of Mars and its rings. Hundreds of millions of small, stylized basketball court icons are animated to cover a significant portion of the ring system's surface area.
A close-up shot revealing the rings are composed of countless icy and rocky debris particles, swirling and shimmering around Mars.

scenes.text: Why did Mars lose its rings? We don't know.
scenes.visual: The magnificent rings around Mars slowly fade out and disappear, leaving Mars alone and ringless as it slowly rotates. A subtle, large question mark appears behind the planet before fading away.
